sentence: Through the mist-laden valley the solitary figure wandered aimlessly
explanation: Literary fronting: the prepositional phrase <Through the mist-laden valley> comes before the subject.

sentence: So profound was her grief that words failed to capture its depth
hint: So + adjective + was + subject + that clause
explanation: Literary inversion with <So ... that> for dramatic emphasis.

sentence: Beneath the ancient oak tree lay the remnants of a forgotten civilisation
explanation: Literary inversion: <Beneath ... lay the remnants> places the setting before the subject.

sentence: Not until the final chapter does the true nature of the protagonist emerge
hint: Not until + noun phrase + auxiliary + subject + verb
explanation: Literary inversion after <Not until> creates suspense.

sentence: In the gathering twilight the old house seemed to breathe with memories
explanation: Literary fronting: the time phrase creates atmosphere before the main clause.

sentence: Gone were the days of innocence replaced by a weary resignation
hint: Past participle + were + subject + past participle phrase
explanation: Literary inversion: <Gone were the days> is a classic literary construction.

sentence: With each passing season the garden surrendered another fragment of its former glory
explanation: Literary fronting: <With each passing season> sets the temporal frame.

sentence: Seldom had the village witnessed such an extraordinary display of courage
hint: Seldom + had + subject + past participle + object
explanation: Literary inversion after <Seldom> for dramatic emphasis.

sentence: Along the winding river path she walked lost in contemplation of the past
hint: Prepositional phrase + subject + verb + participial phrase
explanation: Literary fronting: the setting phrase precedes the character's action.

sentence: Such was the power of his words that even his adversaries were moved to silence
explanation: Literary inversion with <Such ... that> for rhetorical emphasis.

sentence: From the ashes of despair there arose an unexpected and luminous hope
explanation: Literary inversion: <there arose> with fronted prepositional phrase.

sentence: Never again would the world seem quite so simple or so full of promise
hint: Never again + would + subject + verb + complement
explanation: Literary inversion after <Never again> for elegiac tone.
